content:
# Exodus Chapter 21 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~44v–45r (PDF pp. 87–88); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 63–64 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_021_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_021_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_021_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_021_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Ordinances: Introduction (v. 1)

**1** And these are the ordinances [**ve'elleh hammishpatim**] that you shall set [**asher tasim**] before them [**lifneyhem**].

---

## Laws Concerning Hebrew Slaves (vv. 2–6)

**2** When you buy [**ki tiqneh**] a Hebrew servant [**eved Ivri**], six years [**shesh shanim**] he shall serve [**ya'avod**], and in the seventh [**uvasshevi'it**] he shall go out free [**yetse lachofshi**] for nothing [**chinnam**].

**3** If he comes in [**im-begappo yavo**] by himself [**begappo**], he shall go out [**yetse**] by himself [**begappo**]; if he is the husband of a wife [**im-ba'al ishah hu**], then his wife [**veyatse'ah ishto**] shall go out with him [**immo**].

**4** If his master [**im-adonav**] gives him [**yitten-lo**] a wife [**ishah**], and she bears him [**veyaldah-lo**] sons [**vanim**] or daughters [**o vanot**], the wife and her children [**ha'ishah viyladeha**] shall belong to her master [**tihyeh la'adoneha**], and he shall go out [**vehu yetse**] by himself [**begappo**].

**5** But if the servant [**ve'im-amor yomar ha'eved**] plainly says [**amor yomar**], 'I love [**ahavti**] my master [**et-adoni**], my wife [**et-ishti**], and my children [**ve'et-banay**]; I will not go out [**lo etse**] free [**chofshi**]' —

**6** then his master shall bring him [**vehiggisho adonav**] to God [**el-ha'Elohim**], and he shall bring him [**vehiggisho**] to the door [**el-haddelet**] or to the doorpost [**o el-hammezuzah**], and his master shall pierce [**veratsa adonav**] his ear [**et-ozno**] with an awl [**bammartsea**], and he shall serve him [**va'avado**] forever [**le'olam**].

---

## Laws Concerning Female Servants (vv. 7–11)

**7** And when a man [**vechi-yimkor ish**] sells [**yimkor**] his daughter [**et-bitto**] as a female servant [**le'amah**], she shall not go out [**lo tetse**] as the male servants go out [**ketset ha'avadim**].

**8** If she is displeasing [**im-ra'ah**] in the eyes of [**be'eyney**] her master [**adoneha**], who designated her for himself [**asher lo ye'adah**], then he shall let her be redeemed [**vehefddah**]. He shall have no authority [**lo-yimshol**] to sell her [**lemokrah**] to a foreign people [**le'am nokhri**], since he has dealt deceitfully [**bevig'do**] with her [**vah**].

**9** And if he designates her [**ve'im-livno yi'adenah**] for his son [**livno**], he shall deal with her [**ya'aseh-lah**] according to the ordinance of daughters [**kemishpat habbanot**].

**10** If he takes [**im-acheret**] another wife for himself [**yiqqach-lo**], her food [**she'erah**], her clothing [**kesutah**], and her conjugal rights [**ve'onatah**] he shall not diminish [**lo yigra**].

**11** And if he does not do [**ve'im-shelosh-elleh lo ya'aseh**] these three things for her [**lah**], then she shall go out [**veyatse'ah**] for nothing [**chinnam**], without payment of money [**eyn kasef**].

---

## Capital Offenses (vv. 12–17)

**12** He who strikes [**makkeh**] a man [**ish**] so that he dies [**vamet**] shall surely be put to death [**mot yumat**].

**13** But he who did not lie in wait [**va'asher lo tsadah**], and God [**veha'Elohim**] let it happen to his hand [**innah leyado**], then I will appoint [**vesamti**] for you a place [**maqom**] to which he may flee [**asher yanus shammah**].

**14** But if a man [**vechi-yazid ish**] acts presumptuously [**yazid**] against his neighbor [**al-re'ehu**] to kill him [**lehorg'o**] by cunning [**be'ormah**], from My altar [**me'im mizbechi**] you shall take him [**tiqqachennu**] to die [**lamut**].

**15** And he who strikes [**umakkeh**] his father [**aviv**] or his mother [**ve'immo**] shall surely be put to death [**mot yumat**].

**16** And he who kidnaps [**vegonevish**] a man [**ish**], whether he sells him [**umekhar'o**] or he is found [**venimtsa**] in his hand [**beyado**], shall surely be put to death [**mot yumat**].

**17** And he who curses [**umqallel**] his father [**aviv**] or his mother [**ve'immo**] shall surely be put to death [**mot yumat**].

---

## Laws Concerning Bodily Injury (vv. 18–27)

**18** And when men [**vechi-yerivun anashim**] quarrel [**yerivun**], and one strikes [**vehikkah**] his neighbor [**et-re'ehu**] with a stone [**be'even**] or with a fist [**o ve'egrof**], and he does not die [**velo yamut**] but takes to his bed [**venafal lemishkav**] —

**19** if he rises [**im-yaqum**] and walks about [**vehithalekh**] outside [**bachutz**] on his staff [**al-mish'anto**], then the one who struck him shall be cleared [**veniqqah hammakkeh**]; only [**raq**] he shall pay for [**yitten**] his loss of time [**shivto**] and shall surely have him healed [**verappo yerappeh**].

**20** And when a man [**vechi-yakkeh ish**] strikes [**yakkeh**] his male servant [**et-avdo**] or his female servant [**o et-amato**] with a rod [**bashshevet**], and he dies [**umet**] under his hand [**tachat yado**], he shall surely be avenged [**naqom yinnaqem**].

**21** However [**akh**], if he survives [**im-yom o yomayim ya'amod**] a day or two [**yom o yomayim**], he shall not be avenged [**lo yuqqam**], for he is his property [**ki khas'po hu**].

**22** And when men [**vechi-yinnatsu anashim**] fight [**yinnatsu**] and strike [**venag'fu**] a pregnant woman [**ishah harah**], and her children come out [**veyatse'u yeladeha**], but there is no harm [**velo yihyeh ason**], he shall surely be fined [**anosh ye'anesh**] as the husband of the woman [**ka'asher yashit alav ba'al ha'ishah**] lays upon him [**yashit alav**], and he shall pay [**venatan**] as the judges determine [**biflilim**].

**23** But if there is harm [**ve'im-ason yihyeh**], then you shall give [**venatattah**] life [**nefesh**] for life [**tachat nafesh**].

**24** Eye [**ayin**] for eye [**tachat ayin**], tooth [**shen**] for tooth [**tachat shen**], hand [**yad**] for hand [**tachat yad**], foot [**regel**] for foot [**tachat ragel**],

**25** burn [**keviyyah**] for burn [**tachat keviyyah**], wound [**petsa**] for wound [**tachat patsa**], stripe [**chabbura**] for stripe [**tachat chabbura**].

**26** And when a man [**vechi-yakkeh ish**] strikes [**yakkeh**] the eye [**et-eyn**] of his male servant [**avdo**] or the eye [**o et-eyn**] of his female servant [**amato**] and destroys it [**veshichatah**], he shall let him go [**yeshallechennu**] free [**lachofshi**] on account of [**tachat**] his eye [**eyno**].

**27** And if he knocks out [**ve'im-shen avdo**] a tooth [**shen**] of his male servant [**avdo**] or a tooth [**o-shen**] of his female servant [**amato**], he shall let him go [**yeshallechennu**] free [**lachofshi**] on account of [**tachat**] his tooth [**shinno**].

---

## The Goring Ox (vv. 28–32)

**28** And when an ox [**vechi-yiggach shor**] gores [**yiggach**] a man [**et-ish**] or a woman [**o et-ishah**] and they die [**vamet**], the ox shall surely be stoned [**saqol yissaqel hashshor**], and its flesh [**et-besaro**] shall not be eaten [**velo ye'akhel**]; but the owner [**uva'al**] of the ox [**hashshor**] shall be clear [**naqi**].

**29** But if the ox [**ve'im shor naggach hu**] was one that gored [**naggach**] in times past [**mitmol shilshom**], and its owner [**uvu'ad biv'alav**] has been warned [**vehu'ad**], and he does not guard it [**velo yishmerennu**], and it kills [**vehemit**] a man [**ish**] or a woman [**o ishah**], the ox shall be stoned [**hashshor yissaqel**] and also [**vegam**] its owner [**be'alav**] shall be put to death [**yumat**].

**30** If a ransom [**im-kofer**] is laid [**yushat**] upon him [**alav**], then he shall give [**venatan**] the redemption price of his life [**pidyon nafsho**] according to all [**kekhol**] that is laid [**asher-yushat**] upon him [**alav**].

**31** Whether it gores [**o-ven yiggach**] a son [**ven**] or gores [**o-vat yiggach**] a daughter [**vat**], according to this ordinance [**kammishpat hazzeh**] it shall be done [**ye'aseh**] to him [**lo**].

**32** If the ox gores [**im-eved yiggach hashshor**] a male servant [**eved**] or a female servant [**o amah**], thirty shekels [**kesef sheloshim sheqalim**] of silver [**kesef**] he shall give [**yitten**] to their master [**la'adonav**], and the ox [**vehashshor**] shall be stoned [**yissaqel**].

---

## Laws Concerning Property Damage (vv. 33–36)

**33** And when a man [**vechi-yiftach ish**] opens [**yiftach**] a pit [**bor**], or when a man [**o chi-yikhreh ish**] digs [**yikhreh**] a pit [**bor**] and does not cover it [**velo yekhassennu**], and an ox [**shor**] or a donkey [**o chamor**] falls [**venafal**] into it [**shammah**] —

**34** the owner of the pit [**ba'al habbor**] shall make restitution [**yeshallem**]; he shall give [**kesef yashiv**] money [**kesef**] to its owner [**liv'alav**], and the dead animal [**vehammet**] shall be his [**yihyeh-lo**].

**35** And when a man's ox [**vechi-yiggof shor-ish**] injures [**yiggof**] the ox [**et-shor**] of his neighbor [**re'ehu**] and it dies [**vamet**], then they shall sell [**umakhru**] the living ox [**et-hashshor hachay**] and divide [**vechatsu**] its price [**et-kaspo**], and also the dead one [**vegam et-hammet**] they shall divide [**yechetsu**].

**36** Or if it is known [**o noda**] that the ox [**ki shor naggach hu**] was one that gored [**naggach**] in times past [**mitmol shilshom**], and its owner [**velo yishmerennu be'alav**] has not guarded it [**velo yishmerennu**], he shall surely pay [**shallem yeshallem**] ox for ox [**shor tachat hashshor**], and the dead animal [**vehammet**] shall be his [**yihyeh-lo**].

---

## Translator's Notes

### Key Terms

**mishpatim** (v. 1) — "ordinances" or "judgments." The Hebrew mishpatim (plural of mishpat) derives from the root sh-p-t ("to judge, govern"). These are not moral absolutes like the Decalogue but casuistic laws — case-law formulated in conditional "if...then" structure. The term mishpat encompasses both the judicial decision and the established precedent that results from it. The LXX renders this as dikaiomata ("righteous decrees, ordinances"), a term that emphasizes the justice-content of the laws rather than their judicial origin. The placement of mishpatim immediately after the Decalogue (Exod 20:1-17) and the altar law (20:22-26) is theologically significant: the absolute, apodictic commands of God are now applied to concrete social situations. The transition from "You shall not murder" (20:13) to "He who strikes a man so that he dies shall surely be put to death" (21:12) is the transition from principle to application, from divine speech to human jurisprudence under divine authority.

**eved Ivri** (v. 2) — "Hebrew servant." The term Ivri ("Hebrew") is used in the Pentateuch almost exclusively in contexts of interaction with non-Israelites or in legal-social contexts describing a person's status. The laws of Exodus 21:2-6 assume a situation in which an Israelite has fallen into debt-servitude — not chattel slavery in the modern sense but a bonded labor arrangement with a fixed term of six years. The seventh-year release echoes the Sabbath pattern: six units of work, then rest/freedom. The LXX renders eved Ivri as paida Hebraion ("a Hebrew servant/child"), using pais rather than doulos ("slave"), a distinction that may reflect a softer social category.

**ha'Elohim** (v. 6) — "God" or "the judges." The phrase vehiggisho adonav el-ha'Elohim ("his master shall bring him to God/the judges") is one of the most debated cruxes in Exodus 21. The word Elohim with the article (ha'Elohim) normally means "God," but in juridical contexts (cf. Exod 22:8-9) it may refer to judges who act as God's representatives. The LXX renders this as pros to kritērion tou theou ("to the judgment-seat of God"), combining both senses — the judges who sit at God's tribunal. The formal equivalence rendering retains "God" as the primary sense, since the doorpost-and-awl ceremony has sacral, not merely judicial, overtones: the servant is being consecrated to permanent service at a threshold, a liminal sacred space.

**le'olam** (v. 6) — "forever." This Hebrew term can mean "to the age, in perpetuity" but in the context of servitude law, the rabbinical tradition (b. Qiddushin 15a) understands it as "until the Jubilee year" (Lev 25:40), not literally for all eternity. The formal equivalence rendering preserves the Hebrew term's face value; the limitation to the Jubilee is a later harmonization from Leviticus.

**ason** (vv. 22-23) — "harm" or "fatal injury." This rare Hebrew word occurs only here and in Genesis 42:4, 38; 44:29 (where Jacob fears ason befalling Benjamin). Its precise meaning is debated: it may refer specifically to death (so Targ. Onqelos, the LXX's exeikonismenon, "fully formed"), or to any serious bodily harm. The LXX of v. 22 significantly diverges from the MT here: it reads ean de exeikonismenon ē ("but if it is fully formed"), introducing a distinction based on the developmental stage of the fetus — if the child is "fully formed" (exeikonismenon), the lex talionis applies; if not, only a fine is imposed. This distinction is absent from the MT, which speaks only of ason ("harm") without specifying to whom the harm occurs. The LXX interpretation became enormously influential in early Christian discussions of embryology and ensoulment.

**nefesh tachat nafesh** (v. 23) — "life for life." The lex talionis ("law of retaliation") stated in vv. 23-25 is the most famous legal formula in the Hebrew Bible. The principle is one of proportional equivalence: the punishment must match the injury, no more and no less. This is not a primitive cry for vengeance but a limitation on vengeance — it forbids both excessive retaliation and inadequate justice. The repetitive structure (eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot, burn for burn, wound for wound, stripe for stripe) creates a comprehensive taxonomy of bodily injuries, descending from the most severe (life) to the least (stripe/bruise). The LXX renders this faithfully: psuchēn anti psuchēs, ophthalmon anti ophthalmou, odonta anti odontos, etc.

**kofer** (v. 30) — "ransom." From the root k-p-r ("to cover, atone"), kofer is the price paid to ransom a life that is forfeit. The legal logic is remarkable: v. 29 declares the ox-owner must die, but v. 30 immediately provides an escape — if the victim's family accepts a monetary payment, the owner lives. This is the only capital offense in the Torah that explicitly permits commutation to a monetary penalty. The theological resonance of the k-p-r root connects this legal provision to the broader Israelite system of atonement (kapparah) — the covering of guilt by a substitute payment.

### Grammatical Notes

**amor yomar** (v. 5) — The infinitive absolute (amor) preceding the finite verb (yomar) creates the emphatic construction: "if he plainly says" or "if he indeed declares." This emphatic form underscores the deliberateness required — the servant's choice to remain must be an explicit, public declaration, not a passive acquiescence. The decision to forgo freedom must be spoken aloud.

**mot yumat** (vv. 12, 15, 16, 17) — The Hophal imperfect with infinitive absolute, the standard death-penalty formula, occurs four times in the capital-offense section (vv. 12-17). The fourfold repetition creates a grim litany: murder (v. 12), striking a parent (v. 15), kidnapping (v. 16), cursing a parent (v. 17). Each offense attacks the fundamental structures of Israelite society — life itself, the family hierarchy, personal liberty, and parental honor.

**naqom yinnaqem** (v. 20) — "he shall surely be avenged." The Niphal imperfect with infinitive absolute. The passive voice is significant: the subject of the avenging is unspecified. Who avenges the slave? The community, acting under divine mandate. The verb naqam ("to avenge") is not the same as the judicial mot yumat ("he shall be put to death"); the distinction suggests that the death of a slave at a master's hand, while punishable, falls into a different legal category than the murder of a free person — a distinction that the text records without fully resolving.

**shallem yeshallem** (v. 36) — "he shall surely pay." The Piel infinitive absolute with Piel imperfect creates double emphasis on the obligation of full restitution. The Piel stem of sh-l-m means "to make whole, restore, pay in full" — from the same root as shalom ("peace, wholeness, completeness"). Restitution in Israelite law is fundamentally about restoring wholeness to the injured party, making the broken situation shalem again.

**biflilim** (v. 22) — "as the judges determine" or "by assessment." This rare term (found only here in the Torah) is related to palal ("to judge, assess, intercede") and pliliyyah ("arbitration"). The meaning is that the fine imposed for striking a pregnant woman is not a fixed statutory amount but is determined case by case through judicial assessment. The LXX renders this as meta axiomatos ("with due assessment"), capturing the arbitrational sense.

### Structural Notes

Exodus 21 is the first chapter of the "Book of the Covenant" (sefer habberit, cf. Exod 24:7), the oldest law collection in the Hebrew Bible. The chapter exhibits a clear structural logic: it moves from laws governing persons (vv. 2-27) to laws governing property (vv. 28-36), and within the person-laws, it moves from the least violent (servitude, vv. 2-11) through escalating violence (capital offenses, vv. 12-17; bodily injury, vv. 18-27) before turning to animal and property cases (vv. 28-36).

The casuistic (case-law) form that dominates this chapter — "when" (ki) or "if" (im) followed by a specific scenario and a prescribed legal consequence — stands in sharp contrast to the apodictic (absolute) form of the Decalogue ("You shall / You shall not"). The two forms are complementary: the Decalogue establishes the moral and theological principles; the mishpatim work out those principles in the complex, messy realities of social life. Together they form a single legal revelation, the absolute and the particular given by the same God at the same mountain.

The servant laws (vv. 2-11) are placed first, immediately after the introductory formula, because they address the most fundamental question the newly liberated Israelites would face: what does freedom mean for a people just freed from slavery? The answer is striking — servitude is not abolished but regulated, limited to six years, and hedged with protections. The liberated slaves are not forbidden from having servants; they are forbidden from treating their servants as Pharaoh treated them. The seventh-year release is the Sabbath principle applied to social relations.

The lex talionis (vv. 23-25) is structurally central to the bodily-injury laws, and its placement between the pregnant-woman case (v. 22) and the slave-injury cases (vv. 26-27) is significant. The talionic principle applies to free persons; for slaves, the remedy is not equivalent injury but manumission (vv. 26-27). A slave who loses an eye does not receive the master's eye in return — he receives his freedom. Freedom, for a slave, is the equivalent of bodily wholeness.

The goring-ox laws (vv. 28-36) introduce the concept of negligence liability. The first-time goring (v. 28) results in the ox's death but the owner's acquittal; the repeat goring (v. 29) results in the owner's death, because he knew the danger and failed to act. The legal principle is that knowledge creates responsibility. Ignorance mitigates; negligence after knowledge is culpable. This distinction between innocent harm and negligent harm runs through the entire chapter, from the unintentional killer (v. 13) to the negligent ox-owner (v. 29) to the uncovered pit (v. 33).

### Theological Notes

The placement of social law immediately after theophany is the most theologically significant structural feature of the Sinai narrative. God descends on the mountain in fire and smoke (ch. 19), speaks the Decalogue (ch. 20), and then — without any narrative interlude — moves directly to the regulation of debt-servitude, assault, and property damage. The implication is that these mundane, even sordid, social cases are not beneath divine concern; they are the very content of the divine will for Israel. The God who shakes mountains also adjudicates disputes about goring oxen. There is no separation between the "sacred" and the "civil" in Israelite law; all law is sacred because all law flows from the Sinai revelation.

The slave laws (vv. 2-11) must be read against the backdrop of the Exodus narrative itself. Israel has just been liberated from Egyptian bondage; the first laws they receive concern the treatment of bondservants. The irony is deliberate: the God who freed Israel from slavery does not abolish servitude but transforms it. The Hebrew eved ("servant, slave") who serves for six years and goes free in the seventh reenacts in miniature the pattern of Israel's own experience — long bondage followed by divinely ordained liberation. Every seventh-year manumission is a small Exodus.

The distinction between the unintentional killer (v. 13) and the premeditated murderer (v. 14) introduces the concept of mens rea (guilty mind) into biblical law. For the accidental killer, God Himself "lets it happen to his hand" (innah leyado) — the language of divine sovereignty is used to describe an accident, suggesting that even unintended deaths occur within God's providential ordering. The remedy for such a person is not death but flight to a designated place of refuge (later formalized as the cities of refuge, Num 35:9-34). The premeditated murderer, by contrast, may be dragged from the very altar of God — even the sanctuary provides no asylum for intentional bloodshed.

The law of the pregnant woman (vv. 22-23) has been the subject of immense theological and ethical debate. The MT distinguishes between two outcomes: (1) the premature birth without ason ("harm"), resulting in a fine; and (2) the case where ason occurs, triggering the lex talionis. The critical question — harm to whom? — is not specified in the Hebrew. The LXX's introduction of the formed/unformed distinction (exeikonismenon / mē exeikonismenon) became the basis for patristic and medieval discussions of ensoulment and the moral status of the fetus, but this distinction is an interpretive addition absent from the Hebrew text.

The thirty-shekel compensation for a slave killed by a goring ox (v. 32) is the only fixed monetary penalty in the chapter. The amount — thirty shekels of silver — reappears in Zechariah 11:12-13 as the contemptuous price at which the shepherd (a figure for God Himself) is valued, and in Matthew 26:15 as the price of Judas' betrayal of Jesus. The intertextual echo is profound: the price of a slave becomes the price of the Messiah, and the legal provision of Exodus 21 is transformed into a prophetic indictment.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 21 (pages 63-64) preserves the LXX version with several significant divergences from the MT. At v. 1, the LXX renders hammishpatim as ta dikaiomata ("the ordinances/righteous decrees"), emphasizing the justice-content of the laws. At v. 2, eved Ivri becomes paida Hebraion ("a Hebrew servant"), using pais rather than doulos — a terminological choice that may soften the servitude category. At v. 6, the critical el-ha'Elohim is rendered pros to kritērion tou theou ("to the judgment-seat of God"), a double interpretation that recognizes both the divine and juridical senses. At v. 8, the MT's asher lo ye'adah ("who designated her for himself") is rendered with hē mē antikatēstēsen ("who did not betroth her"), making the betrothal commitment explicit. At v. 13, the MT's veha'Elohim innah leyado ("and God let it happen to his hand") becomes ho de theos paredōken eis tas cheiras autou ("and God delivered [him] into his hands"), using paradidōmi ("to deliver, hand over") — a more active divine role than the MT's causative innah ("caused to happen"). At vv. 22-23, the most significant divergence: the MT's velo yihyeh ason ("and there is no harm") becomes ean de mē exeikonismenon ē ("but if it is not fully formed"), introducing the formed/unformed fetus distinction absent from the Hebrew. This reading, likely influenced by Aristotelian embryology, became foundational for early Christian moral theology. At v. 24, the LXX faithfully renders the lex talionis: ophthalmon anti ophthalmou, odonta anti odontos, cheira anti cheiros, poda anti podos. At v. 32, the LXX renders the thirty shekels as triakonta didrachma ("thirty double-drachmas"), converting the Hebrew monetary unit to a Greek equivalent. Throughout Vaticanus pages 63-64, the text is written in the characteristic three-column continuous uncial script, with chapter 21 beginning on page 63 (visible as a new section beginning in the left column, following the end of chapter 20) and continuing through approximately two-thirds of page 64, where it transitions into chapter 22. The Vaticanus arrangement of the Exodus legal material follows the LXX's distinctive ordering, which at points diverges from the MT sequence (notably in chapters 35-40), though in chapter 21 the verse order is substantially identical.

--- LOGOS
